sutta: The Buddha said monks just as even a trifling bit of dung has an evil smell so likewise do I not favor existing even for a trifling time not even for the lasting of a finger snap and.
commentary:
The Buddha says that he does not want to exist even for a trifling time and existence to him is likened to dung which has a bad smell so that's how he views existence why does the Buddha say that existence is uh so um uh so obnoxious and sort of so foul like uh is because even Buddhas have to suffer because there are two types of suffering one is mental suffering the other one is physical suffering the Buddha said that all ariyas have done away with mental suffering but they still have physical suffering and we cannot run away from physical suffering as long as we have a body because with a body we have uh aging sickening and dying and the 
    
    
    snippet:Buddha said in more than one sutta and when he was old that his his body was breaking up and it gave him a lot of suffering so even Buddhas have to suffer and some people are of the opinion that because Buddhas and arahants don't have self that they do not suffer but if that is the case then you can think for example if the Buddha had physical suffering from the body then if there was no if it did not affect him there would have been no necessity for him to enter into a deep janaza or cessation of perception and feeling um which the Buddha did when the pain of the body was quite unbearable he said he had to enter into deep states of meditation so that he did not experience pain in the body and then after that he comes out of it for some time there is no pain but later the pain might come back again so there's one sutta in the itty but it's 2.44 There the Buddha said what because is the nirvana element with residue left here a biku is an arahant one whose asavas are destroyed the holy life fulfilled who has done what had to be done laid down the burden attained the goal destroyed the fetters of being and is completely released through final knowledge however his five sense faculties remain unimpaired by which he still experiences what is agreeable and disagreeable and he feels pleasure and pain sukha and dukkha it is the extinction of attachment head and delusion in him that is called the nibana element with residue left now what because is the nibana element with no residue left then he explains the other part so here the Buddha is saying because arahanna still has the five sense faculties he feels suka and dukkha so that explains why uh the Buddha says that existence uh he he doesn't want to exist uh even for a short time if he if he uh if he could and this uh you'll find is quite uh a different from some later books we see that the Buddha sort of come back again and again or he's always in existence now the next sutta is.
aud_file: 005_Anguttara Nikaya Book 1D 1184 - 12148 by Bhante Hye Dhammavuddho Mahathera.mp3

quiz.quote: Just as even a trifling bit of dung has an evil smell, so I do not favor existence even for a trifling time.

options.title: Disgust as a mood
options.body: The point is mainly to feel repelled by an unpleasant image.

options.title: Existence as burden
options.body: Even a tiny interval of conditioned existence carries the problem of suffering.

options.title: The body is impure
options.body: The teaching is only about the unattractive nature of the physical body.

options.title: Short practice is enough
options.body: A finger-snap of practice is sufficient to finish the path.

quiz.teacherSummary: The teacher is pointing to the burden of conditioned existence itself. Even a tiny interval of becoming carries dukkha, so the image is not merely about disgust; it is about seeing why the path aims beyond continued existence.

sutta: The Buddha said even as monks in this rose apple land that is Jambudīpa trifling in number are the pleasant parks the pleasant groves the pleasant grounds and lakes while more numerous are the steep precipitous places unfoldable rivers dense tickets of stakes and thorns and inaccessible mountains just so few in number are those beings that welcome when they hear it the Dhamma Vinaya set forth by a Tathāgata more numerous are they that do not.
commentary:
Here the Buddha is saying that in india the accessible places where people can use are very much less than those places which are inaccessible like uh steep places mountains uh dense uh forest and jungle unfoldable rivers etc you must remember this sutta was spoken two thousand five hundred years ago so the number uh the the amount of inaccessible places was much more during that time than now and the Buddha said that few people and just like the the the the amount of places that are accessible so uh small amount compared to the whole of india so in the same way few people uh out of the many one to listen to Dhamma Vinaya Dhamma Vinaya is teaches us the truth or reality about existence and it is actually painful to hear the reality of existence and sometimes people don't want to face the truth they want to face their reality they would like to believe what they want to believe what they like to believe so because of that few people want to listen to the Dhamma and the disciplinary code taught by the Buddha the Vinaya so in other words we don't expect to have 
    
    snippet:many followers of the Buddha's teaching in fact most Buddhas when they are enlightened do not want to teach just like our Buddha Buddha after he was enlightened he contemplated the minds of living beings and he found that few people are inclined to listen to the Dhamma and even if they listen few uh even fewer want to practice it so he actually wanted to be a pachika Buddha but later he was persuaded by maha this uh brahma sahampati to turn the wheel of the Dhamma and he did surah we find this happens more more than once that sometimes the Buddha is not inclined to do something um somebody appeals to him to change his mind sometimes they appear once he doesn't want to change his mind then they appeal a second time still he doesn't want to change his mind but when they appeal the third time out of compassion he will change his mind and do something that at first he was not inclined to do so this shows uh that actually pachika Buddhas are able to teach but it's just that they are not inclined to teach it's not like some of the later books I say that particular Buddhas are unable to teach because whatever path that they have walked through is very clear to them the path that they have walked through so it shouldn't be uh difficult at all for them to teach and they would rather teach actually by example they show a good example of being a renuncian and they practice and perhaps people if people ask them in individually of the dharma they might teach but they are not inclined to teach to the whole world and turn the dharma wheel and some people think that that is selfishness but it is not selfishness because it appears to me that every being has its own time and the best teacher is suffering because when we suffer enough and it will bring us to walk the path if we have not been in samsara we are not suffered enough we are not spiritually mature even if the Buddha is around we would not be inclined to follow his teaching so that was that is why we find that during the Buddha's time there were many people who did not want to follow the Buddha's teachings even though the Buddha was in the world now the next is.
aud_file: 005_Anguttara Nikaya Book 1D 1184 - 12148 by Bhante Hye Dhammavuddho Mahathera.mp3

quiz.quote: Few are the pleasant parks and lakes; more numerous are steep places, thickets, and inaccessible mountains. So too, few welcome the Dhamma-Vinaya.

options.title: Receptivity is rare
options.body: Few beings are ready to welcome a teaching that asks them to face reality.

options.title: Nature is the teacher
options.body: The main lesson is that wilderness is better than towns and parks.

options.title: Ancient geography
options.body: The passage is primarily a survey of Jambudipa's terrain.

options.title: Many paths are equal
options.body: The teaching says most approaches are equally accessible.

quiz.teacherSummary: Few people welcome the Dhamma-Vinaya because it asks them to face the truth of existence, and that truth can be painful to hear. Most prefer what they like to believe, so even when a Buddha teaches, few listen deeply and fewer still practise.

sutta: The Buddha says just as monks in this Jambudīpa rose apple land that is india trifling in number are the pleasant parks the pleasant groves the pleasant grounds and lakes while more numerous other steep precipitous places unfordable rivers dense thickets of stakes and thorns and inaccessible mountains just so few in number are those beings who diseasing as men are reborn among men more numerous are those beings who diseasing as men are reborn in hell who are reborn in the wombs of animals who are reborn in the realm of ghosts just so few in number are those beings who deceasing as men are reborn among the devas more numerous are those beings who deceasing as men are reborn in hell in the wombs of animals in the realm of ghosts few in number are those beings who diseasing as devas are reborn as devas or are reborn as men more numerous are those beings who deceasing as devas are born into the three woeful planes of hell the animal realm and the realm of ghosts similarly few in number are those beings who deceasing from hell or from the animal realm or from the ghost realm are reborn as men or as devas more numerous are those beings who deceasing from hell from the animal realm from the ghost realm are reborn back into the hell realm or in the animal realm or in the ghost realm.
commentary: So basically what this is saying is that most men after they pass away they are not they don't come back into the human realm or go up into the deva realm instead most of them go into the warfoot plains the three woeful planes hell animal realm and the ghost realm similarly devas most of the devas when they pass away are reborn into the woeful plains few of them are reborn backed as devas or as human beings and even the beings from the woeful planes after they pass away most of them are reborn back into the woefu planes and not into the human realm or the heavenly realm now what come back into the human realm human world or are reborn as devas maybe seven or eight out of ten people go down into the whole food plains so from there we can realize that it is quite uh dangerous uh and uh we should not be like most people most ordinary people now they live a very ordinary life and then they go into the uh woeful planes because they follow their inclinations they follow all their lower instincts it would appear like the number of beings in the in the in the world every world system is like a pyramid you know the higher you go the less beings there are the lower you go the more beings there are the base is very wide the top is very narrow now the other thing here you can see here is that then the woefu planes mentioned here and throughout the suttas are only three you know that is the first one the lowest one is the health realm and slightly above that is the animal realm and and better than the animal realm according to samsuta is the realm of ghosts so there are only three planes of existence in the woefu planes later books added another plane called the asura plane but when we check with the suttas we find that it contradicts the sutta because in the sutta two suttas in the diga nikaya one says that the lowest type of asuras are the kalakanja asuras and then later in another sutta it is stated that the kala kanja asuras are also devas so you can see here that if the lowest kind of asuras are devas then all of the asuras must be devasta so there are only three woeful planes and not four.
aud_file: 005_Anguttara Nikaya Book 1D 1184 - 12148 by Bhante Hye Dhammavuddho Mahathera.mp3

quiz.quote: Few beings are reborn among humans or devas; more numerous are those reborn in lower destinations.

options.title: A cosmic threat
options.body: The passage is mainly meant to frighten people with punishment.

options.title: Urgency of practice
options.body: Human and deva rebirth are rare, so heedlessness is dangerous.

options.title: Fate is fixed
options.body: Most beings must fall because their future cannot be changed.

options.title: Devas are safe
options.body: Once born among devas, beings are secure from lower rebirth.

quiz.teacherSummary: Most beings do not return to fortunate destinations after death. Humans and devas often fall into the woeful planes, and beings in those planes usually remain there. That makes heedless ordinary life dangerous and gives practice real urgency.

aud_file: 005_Anguttara Nikaya Book 1D 1184 - 12148 by Bhante Hye Dhammavuddho Mahathera.mp3

quiz.quote: These are to be reckoned as gains: forest dwelling, alms, rag robes, teaching Dhamma, knowledge, contentment, and freedom from sickness.

options.title: Worldly success
options.body: Gain means status, family, appearance, and a large following.

options.title: Supports for practice
options.body: The real gains are conditions that simplify life and strengthen training.

options.title: Reject community
options.body: The teaching says followers and teaching are obstacles to practice.

options.title: Health alone matters
options.body: Freedom from sickness is the only gain the Buddha is praising.

quiz.teacherSummary: These are real gains because they support the holy life: seclusion, alms, simple robes, teaching Dhamma, Vinaya, knowledge, good conduct, contentment, pleasant speech, and health. They simplify life, wear down ego, and help the Dhamma continue.

sutta: Monks if even for the time of a finger snap a monk develops the first jhāna he is called a monk who does not dwell devoid of jhāna he carries out the master's instruction he responds to advice and he does not eat the country's alms food in vain how much more then those who make much of it.
commentary: In this sutta the Buddha is praising among who can attain jhāna even for a short very short time as as short as a finger snap so here firstly we find that the Buddha praises jhāna praises the attainment of chana and this is quite different from uh nowadays some people not belittle jaana the other thing we can see from this is that jhāna can be attained even for a short moment even a person they how long a person can attain jhāna and abide in ghana depends on his how long has cultivated it cultivated in a person is new has just attained jhāna it's not very stable and he could attain it for a short time but with longer practice perhaps he could he can abide in china for longer and longer this jhāna I just like to explain a bit this word jhāna is sometimes translated differently years ago they used to translate it as trans musing etc and more recently they translated it as absorption and mental absorption but the word jhāna literally means incandescence bright brightness so probably translation which is nearer the root word now would be a state of mental incandescence because a person who can attain jhāna his mind becomes bright that's why when it's reborn into the jhāna plane it becomes a being with a lot of brightness now we go to the next suta.
aud_file: 005_Anguttara Nikaya Book 1D 1184 - 12148 by Bhante Hye Dhammavuddho Mahathera.mp3

quiz.quote: If even for the time of a finger snap a monk develops the first jhana, he does not dwell devoid of jhana.

options.title: Small collectedness matters
options.body: Even brief genuine collectedness fulfills the direction of the training.

options.title: Instant liberation
options.body: A single finger-snap of jhana means the path is complete.

options.title: Alms as reward
options.body: The main point is that monks earn food by performing a ritual.

options.title: Duration is all
options.body: Only long absorptions matter; brief practice is dismissed.

quiz.teacherSummary: The Buddha praises even a finger-snap of genuine first jhana. A new jhana may last only briefly, but with cultivation it becomes steadier. The point is that collectedness is valuable even in a small, real moment.

sutta: Monks those who do not partake of mindfulness of the body do not partake of the deathless monks those who partake of mindfulness of the body partake of the deathless.
commentary: So here the Buddha is saying that mindfulness of the body is extremely important it can bring us to the deathless state but here it uh it is my personal opinion that body here includes the mind also body sometimes because there's some other uh where the Buddha mentions the body with the perceptions and mental workings so here even though it doesn't mention perception and mental workings I would assume that it includes the mental partner the mind because the body and the mind are the two compo two things that we associate with the cell the Buddha said uh we always associate the cell with the five kandas and the five kandas if you analyze it now it is actually the body and full mental parts which constitute the mind.
aud_file: 005_Anguttara Nikaya Book 1D 1184 - 12148 by Bhante Hye Dhammavuddho Mahathera.mp3

sutta: Bhikkhus, I do not see even a single thing that changes so quickly as the mind.
commentary: The teacher highlights that the mind can change extremely fast. Use this wisely: act quickly when a good intention arises, and pause when a harmful intention (like anger) arises, because the state will change if you don't act on it.
aud_file: 003_Anguttara Nikaya Book 1B 15 - 11014 by Bhante Hye Dhammavuddho Mahathera.mp3
